---
title: "Lab Report Writing Guide"
canvas:
  type: page
  published: true
---

## Writing Lab Reports in Quarto

For the initial lab sessions (Labs 1–9) in this course, you will submit a short **lab report** as a rendered PDF. We use [Quarto](https://quarto.org) to write reports — it lets you combine text, equations, figures, and code in a single document.

For a complete guide on writing reports in Quarto, see:

**[Writing Documentation with Quarto](https://python.ju.se/ProgrammingFundamentals/writing_documentation.html)**

## Installing development tools

Run this command in PowerShell to install the development tools

```powershell
irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cenmir/python-dev-installer/main/download.ps1 | iex
```


### Getting Started

Your report is written as a `.qmd` file (or a Jupyter notebook `.ipynb`) and rendered to PDF. A minimal report looks like this:

````markdown
---
title: "Lab 1 – LED from Power Supply"
author: "Alice Andersson, Bob Bengtsson, Charlie Carlsson"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
    fontsize: 11pt
    number-sections: true
---

## Purpose

Briefly state the goal of the lab in your own words.

## Theory

Explain the relevant theory. Use equations where appropriate:

$$V = IR$$

$$P = VI = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$$

## Method

Describe what you did. Include circuit diagrams (photos or drawn).

## Results

Present your measurements in tables and/or figures.

| Resistor | Calculated $I$ | Measured $I$ | Deviation |
|----------|---------------|-------------|-----------|
| 470 Ω    | 21.3 mA       | 20.8 mA     | 2.3%      |

## Discussion

- Do your measurements match the calculations?
- What sources of error exist?
- What did you learn?

## Conclusion

One or two sentences summarizing the key takeaway.
````

### Render to PDF

```bash
quarto render my_report.qmd --to pdf
```

### What We Expect

Your report should be **short** — typically 2–4 pages. Focus on:

1. **Showing your calculations** — use proper equations, not just numbers
2. **Presenting measurements** — tables comparing calculated vs measured values
3. **Answering the lab questions** — integrate answers into your discussion
4. **Including circuit photos** — a photo of your breadboard setup, or a drawn circuit diagram

You do **not** need to write a lengthy introduction or extensive background theory. Keep it concise.

### Including Images

Take photos of your circuits and oscilloscope screens. Include them in your report:

```markdown
![My circuit on the breadboard](photos/lab1_circuit.jpg){width=60%}
```

### Tips

- All three group members should be listed as authors
- Number your sections (`number-sections: true`) for easy reference
- Use `$$` for display equations and `$` for inline math
- Keep file sizes reasonable — resize photos before including them
